title: EHRC Employment Statutory Code of Practice
chapters: Statutory Code of Practice · Issued under Equality Act 2006 s.14 · Approved by Parliament · In effect 6 April 2011 · 1,172 paragraphs · Covers all protected characteristics in the employment context
desc: The EHRC Employment Statutory Code of Practice is the authoritative guide to the application of the Equality Act 2010 in the workplace. It is a statutory code — employment tribunals must take it into account where relevant (Equality Act 2006 s.15(4)). The Code covers all nine protected characteristics (age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex, sexual orientation), all forms of prohibited conduct (direct discrimination, indirect discrimination, harassment, victimisation, failure to make reasonable adjustments), and their application throughout the employment lifecycle (recruitment, terms and conditions, promotion, transfer, training, dismissal, post-employment). The Code explains the employer's vicarious liability for acts of employees and agents (EqA 2010 s.109) and the 'all reasonable steps' defence (s.109(4)). It covers the burden of proof (s.136), the obligation to make reasonable adjustments for disabled workers (ss.20–21), positive action provisions (s.158–159), and occupational requirements (Schedule 9). At 1,172 paragraphs across 334 pages, it is the most comprehensive employment law code of practice in the UK.
legalStatus: Statutory Code of Practice issued under Equality Act 2006 s.14. Employment tribunals must take it into account when determining any question arising under the Equality Act 2010 (Equality Act 2006 s.15(4)). Failure to follow the Code does not in itself make a person liable to proceedings, but compliance or non-compliance is admissible as evidence.

sections.title: Direct discrimination, indirect discrimination, and combined discrimination
sections.summary: Direct discrimination (EqA 2010 s.13) occurs when a person treats another less favourably because of a protected characteristic than they treat or would treat others. The comparator must be in the same or not materially different circumstances. Direct discrimination cannot be justified (except for age discrimination, where it can be justified as a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim). Indirect discrimination (s.19) occurs when a provision, criterion or practice (PCP) that applies to everyone puts people with a particular protected characteristic at a particular disadvantage, and the PCP cannot be shown to be a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim. The Code provides extensive guidance on what constitutes a PCP, how to identify the pool for comparison, what constitutes 'particular disadvantage', and how the justification defence operates. The Code explains that the concept of 'because of' the protected characteristic includes cases where the characteristic is the reason for the treatment, even if the decision-maker does not consciously have the characteristic in mind (subconscious discrimination).
sections.text:
Direct discrimination (s.13): A person (A) discriminates against another (B) if, because of a protected characteristic, A treats B less favourably than A treats or would treat others.

Key principles from the Code:
• The comparator must be in the same or not materially different circumstances (s.23(1))
• A hypothetical comparator may be used where no actual comparator exists
• 'Because of' includes cases where the characteristic is one of the reasons, even if not the only or main reason
• Direct discrimination includes perception discrimination (treating someone less favourably because you perceive they have a characteristic, even if they don't) and associative discrimination (treating someone less favourably because of their association with someone who has the characteristic)
• Direct age discrimination can be justified as a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim (s.13(2))

Indirect discrimination (s.19): A person (A) discriminates against another (B) if A applies to B a provision, criterion or practice (PCP) which is discriminatory in relation to a relevant protected characteristic of B's.

A PCP is discriminatory if:
(a) A applies, or would apply, it to persons with whom B does not share the characteristic
(b) it puts, or would put, persons with whom B shares the characteristic at a particular disadvantage compared with persons with whom B does not share it
(c) it puts, or would put, B at that disadvantage
(d) A cannot show it to be a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim

versions.description: Code issued alongside commencement of the Equality Act 2010. Replaced the previous codes issued under the Sex Discrimination Act 1975, Race Relations Act 1976, and Disability Discrimination Act 1995.

leadingCases.name: Nagarajan v London Regional Transport [1999] UKHL 36
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the decision-maker need not be consciously motivated by the protected characteristic. Subconscious discrimination is sufficient. If the characteristic has a significant influence on the decision, the treatment is 'because of' the characteristic even if the decision-maker is not aware of it.
leadingCases.significance: Established that subconscious motivation is sufficient for direct discrimination — the decision-maker need not be aware of their discriminatory reasoning

leadingCases.name: Essop v Home Office [2017] UKSC 27
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that in indirect discrimination claims, the claimant does not need to explain why the PCP puts the group at a particular disadvantage. It is sufficient to show that the PCP does in fact do so. The reason why the group is disadvantaged is irrelevant to establishing the claim.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority clarifying that no causal explanation is needed for the group disadvantage in indirect discrimination

leadingCases.name: Homer v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire [2012] UKSC 15
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court considered the justification defence for indirect age discrimination. The legitimate aim must be sufficiently important to justify limiting a fundamental right, the measure must be rationally connected to the aim, and the means chosen must be no more than necessary to accomplish it.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority on the proportionality test for justifying indirect discrimination — the three-stage test

refs: EqA 2010 s.23 (comparison — circumstances must not be materially different)
EqA 2010 s.136 (burden of proof — two-stage shift)

sections.title: Reasonable adjustments for disabled workers
sections.summary: The duty to make reasonable adjustments (EqA 2010 ss.20–21) requires the employer to take reasonable steps to avoid a substantial disadvantage caused by a provision, criterion or practice (PCP), a physical feature of premises, or the lack of an auxiliary aid or service. The duty is anticipatory — the employer must consider adjustments proactively, not wait for the disabled worker to request them. The Code provides extensive guidance on what constitutes a 'substantial disadvantage' (more than minor or trivial), what adjustments may be reasonable (considering effectiveness, practicability, cost, employer's resources, and availability of external assistance), and examples of common adjustments (flexible working, modified duties, reallocation of tasks, physical alterations, provision of equipment, extra supervision, time off for treatment). The duty applies at all stages: recruitment, employment, and dismissal. Failure to make reasonable adjustments is a form of discrimination — it is not a defence that the employer did not know the worker was disabled if they could reasonably have been expected to know.
sections.text:
The three requirements of the reasonable adjustment duty (EqA 2010 s.20):

First requirement: Where a PCP puts a disabled person at a substantial disadvantage in comparison with persons who are not disabled, the employer must take such steps as it is reasonable to have to take to avoid the disadvantage.

Second requirement: Where a physical feature puts a disabled person at a substantial disadvantage, the employer must take such steps as it is reasonable to have to take to avoid the disadvantage.

Third requirement: Where a disabled person would, but for the provision of an auxiliary aid, be put at a substantial disadvantage, the employer must take such steps as it is reasonable to have to take to provide the auxiliary aid.

Factors relevant to reasonableness (from the Code):
• the extent to which taking the step would prevent the disadvantage
• the practicability of the step
• the financial and other costs of making the adjustment and the extent of any disruption
• the extent of the employer's financial and other resources
• the availability to the employer of financial or other assistance (e.g. Access to Work)
• the nature of the employer's activities and the size of the undertaking
• the type of employer — the Code expects larger and better-resourced employers to do more

leadingCases.name: Environment Agency v Rowan [2008] ICR 218
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT established a structured approach to reasonable adjustment claims: (1) identify the PCP, physical feature, or lack of auxiliary aid; (2) identify the non-disabled comparators; (3) identify the nature and extent of the substantial disadvantage suffered by the claimant. Only then can the tribunal assess what adjustments the employer should reasonably have made.
leadingCases.significance: Established the structured analytical framework for reasonable adjustment claims

leadingCases.name: Archibald v Fife Council [2004] UKHL 32
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the duty to make reasonable adjustments may require the employer to treat a disabled person more favourably than others. This includes transferring the person to a different role without competitive interview if they can no longer perform their existing role due to disability. The duty goes beyond simply removing barriers — it may require positive steps.
leadingCases.significance: House of Lords authority that reasonable adjustments may require treating a disabled person more favourably than others

refs: EqA 2010 ss.20–21 (duty to make adjustments and failure to comply)
EqA 2010 s.6 and Schedule 1 (definition of disability — substantial and long-term adverse effect on day-to-day activities)
EqA 2010 s.15 (discrimination arising from disability — employer must know or ought reasonably to know of disability)
Access to Work scheme (DWP — government funding for workplace adjustments)

sections.title: Employer liability: vicarious liability and the 'all reasonable steps' defence
sections.summary: An employer is vicariously liable for acts of discrimination, harassment, and victimisation carried out by employees in the course of employment (EqA 2010 s.109(1)), and for acts of agents done with the employer's authority (s.109(2)). 'In the course of employment' is interpreted broadly — it includes acts at work, at work-related social events, and on work trips. The employer has a defence if it can show that it took 'all reasonable steps' to prevent the employee from doing the act complained of, or from doing acts of that description (s.109(4)). The Code explains that 'all reasonable steps' includes having an adequate equality and diversity policy, communicating the policy to all staff, providing effective training (that is not stale — Allay v Gehlen [2021]), implementing the policy consistently, taking prompt and effective action when complaints arise, and reviewing the effectiveness of measures. Having a policy alone is not sufficient — the policy must be communicated, enforced, and staff must be trained on it (Canniffe v East Riding [2000]).
sections.text:
Employer vicarious liability (EqA 2010 s.109):

(1) Anything done by a person (A) in the course of A's employment must be treated as also done by the employer.
(2) Anything done by an agent for a principal, with the authority of the principal, must be treated as also done by the principal.
(3) It does not matter whether the thing is done with the employer's or principal's knowledge or approval.
(4) In proceedings against A's employer (B), it is a defence for B to show that B took all reasonable steps to prevent A from doing that thing, or from doing anything of that description.

The 'all reasonable steps' defence requires the employer to demonstrate:
• an adequate written policy prohibiting discrimination and harassment
• effective communication of the policy to all employees
• regular, effective training on the policy — training must be current, not stale
• consistent enforcement of the policy — complaints must be taken seriously and acted upon
• monitoring and reviewing the effectiveness of the measures
• taking prompt and proportionate action when issues arise

leadingCases.name: Allay (UK) Ltd v Gehlen [2021] ICR 645
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that stale training delivered two years previously was not sufficient to establish the 'all reasonable steps' defence. Where harassment occurred despite previous training, and the training had not been refreshed, the employer could not demonstrate that it had taken all reasonable steps. Training must be current and effective.
leadingCases.significance: Key recent authority — stale equality training is insufficient for the 'all reasonable steps' defence

leadingCases.name: Jones v Tower Boot Co Ltd [1997] ICR 254
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that 'in the course of employment' under the discrimination legislation should be given its everyday meaning, not the narrower common law test used in tort. An employer can be vicariously liable for racial harassment committed by one employee against another even if the acts were not authorised or part of the employee's job.
leadingCases.significance: Established the broad interpretation of 'in the course of employment' for employer vicarious liability in discrimination cases

refs: EqA 2010 s.109 (employer liability — vicarious liability and 'all reasonable steps' defence)
EqA 2010 s.110 (personal liability of employees who commit discriminatory acts)
EqA 2010 s.40A (proactive duty to prevent sexual harassment — from 26 October 2024)
Canniffe v East Riding of Yorkshire Council [2000] (policy alone insufficient — communication, training, enforcement required)

pendingAmendments: EqA 2010 s.40A preventive duty IN FORCE 26 October 2024
ERA 2025 — 'all reasonable steps' standard + third-party harassment (not yet in force)
ERA 2025 — equality action plans for 250+ employers (not yet in force)

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Statutory Code of Practice — approved by resolution of both Houses of Parliament
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Employment tribunals and courts must take the Code into account when it appears relevant to any question arising in proceedings (Equality Act 2006 s.15(4)). The Code is admissible in evidence. Failure to follow it is not in itself unlawful but may be taken into account.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The EHRC Employment Code is the single most authoritative guidance on the Equality Act 2010 as it applies to employment. It replaces the previous statutory codes issued by the Equal Opportunities Commission, Commission for Racial Equality, and Disability Rights Commission. The Code covers all protected characteristics, direct and indirect discrimination, harassment, victimisation, the duty to make reasonable adjustments, positive action, occupational requirements, and employer vicarious liability.

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2010
precedingDocuments.significance: The governing statute. The Code provides detailed guidance on the Act's application to employment.

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2006 s.14
precedingDocuments.significance: The statutory power under which the EHRC issues Codes of Practice.

leadingCases.name: Essop v Home Office [2017] UKSC 27
leadingCases.heldText: For indirect discrimination, the claimant need not explain why the PCP puts their group at a particular disadvantage — only that it does. Statistical evidence of group disadvantage is sufficient.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court — no need to explain 'why' the PCP disadvantages the group.

leadingCases.name: Aster Communities v Akerman-Mayall [2015] ICR 72
leadingCases.heldText: The duty to make reasonable adjustments requires the employer to take such steps as are reasonable to avoid substantial disadvantage. The employer must consider each case individually.
leadingCases.significance: Individual assessment requirement for reasonable adjustments.

leadingCases.name: Allay (UK) Ltd v Gehlen [2021] ICR 645
leadingCases.heldText: Stale training is insufficient for the 'all reasonable steps' defence. Training must be current and effective.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on employer vicarious liability defence.
